name: Government
description: A study of American government and political systems — the Constitution, three branches, civil liberties, political participation, public policy, and comparative government with global perspectives.

modules.title: Foundations of Government
modules.objectives: Explain the purposes of government; compare political philosophies of Locke, Hobbes, and Rousseau; identify major types of government.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy Civics — Purpose of Government
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — U.S. Government and Civics lesson videos for this topic: Foundations of Government.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy Civics — Purposes of Government practice
modules.assignment: Compare Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau on two dimensions: the nature of humans in the state of nature and the ideal form of government. Which philosopher most influenced American democracy and why?

modules.title: The U.S. Constitution
modules.objectives: Describe the Constitutional Convention; explain the structure of the Constitution; analyze the amendment process and federalism.
modules.readingNote: Congress.gov — Full Text of the U.S. Constitution
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — U.S. Government and Civics lesson videos for this topic: The U.S. Constitution.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy Civics — The Constitution practice
modules.assignment: Summarize the three main compromises made at the Constitutional Convention (Great Compromise, Three-Fifths Compromise, Commerce Compromise). How did each shape the structure of U.S. government?

modules.title: The Bill of Rights & Civil Liberties
modules.objectives: Identify the freedoms protected by the First Amendment; explain due process and equal protection; analyze landmark Supreme Court civil liberties cases.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — U.S. Government and Civics lesson videos for this topic: The Bill of Rights & Civil Liberties.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy Civics — Civil Liberties practice
modules.assignment: Choose two landmark Supreme Court cases that expanded or limited civil liberties (e.g., Tinker v. Des Moines, Engel v. Vitale). For each case, explain the facts, the ruling, and its lasting impact on American society.

modules.title: The Legislative Branch
modules.objectives: Describe the bicameral structure of Congress; trace the lawmaking process; explain the role of committees and checks on Congress.
modules.readingNote: Congress.gov — Learn About the Legislative Process
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — U.S. Government and Civics lesson videos for this topic: The Legislative Branch.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy Civics — Legislative Branch practice
modules.assignment: Trace an actual bill through the legislative process using Congress.gov. Identify the sponsor, committee(s), key votes, and whether it became law. Explain one obstacle it faced along the way.

modules.title: The Executive Branch
modules.objectives: Identify presidential powers enumerated in the Constitution; explain the role of the Cabinet and executive agencies; analyze the use and limits of executive orders.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy Civics — Powers of the President
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — U.S. Government and Civics lesson videos for this topic: The Executive Branch.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy Civics — Executive Branch practice
modules.assignment: Research one executive order issued in the past 20 years. Describe its purpose, the authority the president claimed, and any legal or political challenges it faced. Was it constitutional? Defend your answer.

modules.title: The Judicial Branch
modules.objectives: Describe the structure of the federal court system; explain judicial review; analyze landmark Supreme Court decisions and their impact.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy Civics — The Supreme Court
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — U.S. Government and Civics lesson videos for this topic: The Judicial Branch.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy Civics — Judicial Branch practice
modules.assignment: Analyze Marbury v. Madison (1803). Explain the background dispute, Chief Justice Marshall's reasoning for establishing judicial review, and why this decision remains foundational to American government.

modules.title: Federalism
modules.objectives: Distinguish enumerated, reserved, and concurrent powers; compare federal vs. state authority; explain the evolution of federalism from dual to cooperative to new federalism.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy Civics — Federalism in the United States
modules.assignment: Select a current policy issue (e.g., marijuana legalization, minimum wage, immigration enforcement). Explain the conflict between federal and state authority on this issue and take a position on which level of government should have primary control.

modules.title: Political Parties & Elections
modules.objectives: Explain the origin and role of the two-party system; compare Democratic and Republican platforms; describe the primary process and the Electoral College.
modules.videoNote: Crash Course Government & Politics #37 — Electoral College
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy Civics — Political Parties practice
modules.assignment: Compare the current Democratic and Republican party platforms on two domestic policy issues (e.g., healthcare, education). Identify the core values underlying each position. Do you think a third party could succeed in the U.S.? Why or why not?

modules.title: Voting, Campaigns & Political Participation
modules.objectives: Analyze factors affecting voter turnout; explain campaign finance rules; assess the impact of gerrymandering and identify ways citizens engage in civic life.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy Civics — Voting and Political Participation
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — U.S. Government and Civics lesson videos for this topic: Voting, Campaigns & Political Participation.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy Civics — Voting Rights practice
modules.assignment: Research one barrier to voting (e.g., voter ID laws, felony disenfranchisement, polling access). Explain how it affects participation, identify which groups are most impacted, and propose one evidence-based solution.

modules.title: Public Policy Process
modules.objectives: Trace the stages of the policy process — agenda setting, formation, adoption, implementation, and evaluation; apply the model to a real policy example.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy Civics — The Policy-Making Process
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — U.S. Government and Civics lesson videos for this topic: Public Policy Process.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy Civics — Public Policy practice
modules.assignment: Select a public policy enacted in the past decade (e.g., the Affordable Care Act, Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act). Trace it through all five stages of the policy process, identifying key actors and obstacles at each stage. Submit your work as a document, image, or link showing the steps, evidence, and final response.

modules.title: Civil Rights Movements & the Law
modules.objectives: Explain the role of the 14th Amendment in extending rights; analyze landmark civil rights legislation; evaluate the ongoing struggles for equality.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy Civics — Civil Rights Movements
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — U.S. Government and Civics lesson videos for this topic: Civil Rights Movements & the Law.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy Civics — Civil Rights practice
modules.assignment: Compare the strategies used by civil rights leaders in the 1950s–1960s (e.g., nonviolent protest, litigation, legislation) with those used by modern movements (e.g., Black Lives Matter, #MeToo). Which approaches have been most effective at producing legal change? Support your argument with specific evidence.

modules.title: Foreign Policy & National Security
modules.objectives: Identify the tools of U.S. foreign policy; explain the roles of NATO, the UN, and the military; analyze how diplomacy, sanctions, and aid are used to advance national interests.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — U.S. Government and Civics lesson videos for this topic: Foreign Policy & National Security.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy Civics — Foreign Policy practice
modules.assignment: Examine one recent U.S. foreign policy decision (e.g., aid to Ukraine, sanctions on Iran, trade policy with China). Identify which foreign policy tools were used, which branches of government were involved, and evaluate whether the policy achieved its stated goals.

modules.title: Economic Policy & the Budget
modules.objectives: Define fiscal policy; explain how the federal budget is created; distinguish mandatory from discretionary spending; analyze the causes and consequences of the national debt.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Personal Finance lesson videos for this topic: Economic Policy & the Budget.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy Civics — Economic Policy practice
modules.assignment: Review a simplified version of the current federal budget. Identify the three largest areas of spending and three main sources of revenue. Propose one change you would make to reduce the deficit, and anticipate one objection to your proposal.

questions.question: Which philosopher argued that people enter a social contract to escape the brutish 'state of nature'?
questions.answer: Thomas Hobbes
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Thomas Hobbes.

questions.question: The principle of popular sovereignty means that:
options: Government power derives from the consent of the governed
Congress can override any court decision
States have power over the federal government
questions.answer: Government power derives from the consent of the governed
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Government power derives from the consent of the governed.

questions.question: The Great Compromise at the Constitutional Convention created:
options: A bicameral legislature with proportional House seats and equal Senate seats
questions.answer: A bicameral legislature with proportional House seats and equal Senate seats
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: A bicameral legislature with proportional House seats and equal Senate seats.

questions.question: Why was the Bill of Rights added to the Constitution?
options: To protect individual freedoms from government overreach
To create the office of president
To give Congress unlimited power to tax
To replace the Articles of Confederation with a monarchy
questions.answer: To protect individual freedoms from government overreach
questions.explanation: The Bill of Rights lists protections for individual liberties and limits certain government actions.

questions.question: Which First Amendment freedom protects citizens' right to criticize the government?
options: Freedom of the press and speech
questions.answer: Freedom of the press and speech
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Freedom of the press and speech.

questions.question: A bill introduced in the House of Representatives must pass through which body before becoming law?
questions.answer: The Senate
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: The Senate.

questions.question: Which power is exclusively reserved to the states under the Tenth Amendment?
questions.answer: Regulating intrastate commerce and education
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Regulating intrastate commerce and education.

questions.question: Presidential veto power is an example of:
questions.answer: Checks and balances
questions.explanation: A veto lets the president check Congress, while Congress may override a veto with enough votes.

questions.question: The Cabinet members are appointed by the president and must be confirmed by:
questions.answer: The Senate
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: The Senate.

questions.question: Judicial review — the power to strike down unconstitutional laws — was established by:
questions.answer: Marbury v. Madison (1803)
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Marbury v. Madison (1803).

questions.question: Enumerated powers are powers:
options: Shared between federal and state governments
Specifically listed in the Constitution as belonging to Congress
questions.answer: Specifically listed in the Constitution as belonging to Congress
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Specifically listed in the Constitution as belonging to Congress.

questions.question: The Supremacy Clause states that:
options: State constitutions always override federal law
Federal law is the supreme law of the land when made under the Constitution
The president can suspend the Constitution during elections
The Supreme Court writes all federal statutes
questions.answer: Federal law is the supreme law of the land when made under the Constitution
questions.explanation: The Supremacy Clause makes the Constitution and valid federal laws supreme over conflicting state laws.

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'separation of powers' and provide one example of how each branch checks another branch of government.
questions.answer: Separation of powers divides government authority among three branches (legislative, executive, judicial). Examples: Congress passes laws but the president can veto them (legislative checks executive); the president nominates judges but the Senate confirms them (executive checks judicial with Senate's check on executive); the Supreme Court can strike down laws as unconstitutional (judicial checks legislative).
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Separation of powers divides government authority among three branches (legislative, executive, judicial). Examples: Congress passes laws but the president can veto them (legislative checks executive); the president nominates judges but the Senate confirms them (executive checks judicial with Senate's check on executive); the Supreme Court can strike down laws as unconstitutional (judicial checks legislative)..

questions.question: The due process clause of the 14th Amendment has been used to:
options: Apply most Bill of Rights protections to state governments
Grant Congress power to regulate interstate commerce
questions.answer: Apply most Bill of Rights protections to state governments
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Apply most Bill of Rights protections to state governments.

questions.question: Describe the process by which the Constitution can be amended. Why did the Founders make this process difficult?
questions.answer: An amendment requires a two-thirds vote in both chambers of Congress (or a constitutional convention called by two-thirds of state legislatures), then ratification by three-fourths (38) of state legislatures or state conventions. The Founders made it difficult to protect fundamental rights from temporary majorities, ensure broad national consensus, and maintain constitutional stability.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: An amendment requires a two-thirds vote in both chambers of Congress (or a constitutional convention called by two-thirds of state legislatures), then ratification by three-fourths (38) of state legislatures or state conventions. The Founders made it difficult to protect fundamental rights from temporary majorities, ensure broad national consensus, and maintain constitutional stability..

questions.question: John Locke's idea that government should protect life, liberty, and property most directly influenced:
options: The Declaration of Independence's natural-rights language
The Federalist Papers' defense of a strong executive only
The creation of the modern income tax
The Twenty-Sixth Amendment lowering the voting age
questions.answer: The Declaration of Independence's natural-rights language
questions.explanation: Locke's natural-rights theory strongly influenced the Declaration of Independence's language about rights and legitimate government.

questions.question: The Federalist Papers were written to:
options: Argue for a Bill of Rights only
Persuade New Yorkers to ratify the Constitution
questions.answer: Persuade New Yorkers to ratify the Constitution
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Persuade New Yorkers to ratify the Constitution.

questions.question: The exclusionary rule (established in Mapp v. Ohio) states that:
options: Evidence obtained illegally cannot be used in court
Suspects must be informed of their rights before questioning
Citizens cannot be tried twice for the same crime
The government must provide lawyers for those who cannot afford one
questions.answer: Evidence obtained illegally cannot be used in court
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Evidence obtained illegally cannot be used in court.

questions.question: A filibuster in the U.S. Senate is a tactic used to:
options: Delay or block legislation through extended debate
questions.answer: Delay or block legislation through extended debate
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Delay or block legislation through extended debate.

questions.question: Executive orders are issued by the president and generally:
options: Direct executive branch action while remaining subject to constitutional and statutory limits
Can amend the Constitution without approval
Automatically override all Supreme Court decisions
Are written and passed by Congress before the president signs them
questions.answer: Direct executive branch action while remaining subject to constitutional and statutory limits
questions.explanation: Executive orders guide executive branch operations but must stay within constitutional and legal authority and may be challenged in court.

questions.question: The Supreme Court's decision in Brown v. Board of Education (1954) overturned:
options: Plessy v. Ferguson's 'separate but equal' doctrine
questions.answer: Plessy v. Ferguson's 'separate but equal' doctrine
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Plessy v. Ferguson's 'separate but equal' doctrine.

questions.question: An unfunded mandate occurs when:
options: The federal government raises taxes without Congressional approval
Congress requires states to implement a program without providing funds
A state refuses to follow a Supreme Court ruling
The president signs a bill into law without reading it
questions.answer: Congress requires states to implement a program without providing funds
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Congress requires states to implement a program without providing funds.

questions.question: In a primary election, voters typically:
options: Choose between the Democratic and Republican nominees
Select their party's candidate for the general election
questions.answer: Select their party's candidate for the general election
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Select their party's candidate for the general election.

questions.question: Gerrymandering refers to:
options: Drawing electoral district boundaries to favor a party or group
Requiring citizens to register before voting
Counting electoral votes after a presidential election
Holding a primary election before a general election
questions.answer: Drawing electoral district boundaries to favor a party or group
questions.explanation: Gerrymandering is the manipulation of district boundaries for political advantage.

questions.question: Agenda setting in the public policy process refers to:
options: Determining which issues government will address
Evaluating whether a policy achieved its goals
questions.answer: Determining which issues government will address
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Determining which issues government will address.

questions.question: The Civil Rights Act of 1964 prohibited discrimination based on:
options: Race, color, religion, sex, and national origin
questions.answer: Race, color, religion, sex, and national origin
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Race, color, religion, sex, and national origin.

questions.question: The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) is primarily:
options: A collective defense alliance among democratic nations
An agreement to share nuclear weapons technology
questions.answer: A collective defense alliance among democratic nations
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: A collective defense alliance among democratic nations.

questions.question: Mandatory spending in the federal budget includes programs such as:
options: Social Security, Medicare, and Medicaid entitlements
Only foreign aid grants approved each year
Only salaries for members of Congress
questions.answer: Social Security, Medicare, and Medicaid entitlements
questions.explanation: Mandatory spending is required by existing law and includes major entitlement programs such as Social Security, Medicare, and Medicaid.

questions.question: In a parliamentary system, the head of government (prime minister) is:
options: Elected directly by voters in a national election
Appointed by the head of state without legislative input
The leader of the majority party or coalition in the legislature
questions.answer: The leader of the majority party or coalition in the legislature
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: The leader of the majority party or coalition in the legislature.

questions.question: The Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) allows citizens to:
options: Sue the president without congressional approval
questions.answer: Request access to government records
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Request access to government records.

questions.question: Fiscal policy refers to government use of:
options: Taxation and spending to influence the economy
questions.answer: Taxation and spending to influence the economy
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Taxation and spending to influence the economy.

questions.question: The Voting Rights Act of 1965 was designed to:
options: Eliminate discriminatory practices that blocked Black Americans from voting
Require every state to use the same ballot design
questions.answer: Eliminate discriminatory practices that blocked Black Americans from voting
questions.explanation: The Voting Rights Act targeted discriminatory barriers such as literacy tests and other practices used to suppress Black voting rights.

questions.question: Compare the U.S. presidential system with a parliamentary system on two dimensions: how the executive is chosen and how the executive can be removed from office.
questions.answer: In the U.S. presidential system, the president is elected separately by voters (via the Electoral College) for a fixed four-year term and can only be removed through the impeachment process (House impeaches, Senate convicts with two-thirds vote). In a parliamentary system, the prime minister is chosen by the legislature — typically the leader of the majority party — and can be removed through a vote of no confidence at any time, allowing more flexible and responsive governance.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: In the U.S. presidential system, the president is elected separately by voters (via the Electoral College) for a fixed four-year term and can only be removed through the impeachment process (House impeaches, Senate convicts with two-thirds vote). In a parliamentary system, the prime minister is chosen by the legislature — typically the leader of the majority party — and can be removed through a vote of no confidence at any time, allowing more flexible and responsive governance..

questions.question: A democracy index typically measures countries on criteria including:
options: GDP per capita and military strength
Electoral process, civil liberties, government functioning, and political participation
Trade surplus and foreign aid given
Number of political parties and voter registration rates alone
questions.answer: Electoral process, civil liberties, government functioning, and political participation
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Electoral process, civil liberties, government functioning, and political participation.

questions.question: Explain how campaign finance laws (e.g., Citizens United v. FEC) affect political participation and democratic equality.
questions.answer: Citizens United v. FEC (2010) ruled that political spending by corporations and outside groups is protected free speech under the First Amendment, removing limits on independent expenditures. This has allowed wealthy donors and Super PACs to spend unlimited amounts on elections, amplifying the voices of the affluent while potentially drowning out ordinary citizens. Supporters argue it protects free speech; critics contend it creates unequal political influence and leads to corruption or the appearance of corruption, undermining democratic equality.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Citizens United v. FEC (2010) ruled that political spending by corporations and outside groups is protected free speech under the First Amendment, removing limits on independent expenditures. This has allowed wealthy donors and Super PACs to spend unlimited amounts on elections, amplifying the voices of the affluent while potentially drowning out ordinary citizens. Supporters argue it protects free speech; critics contend it creates unequal political influence and leads to corruption or the appearance of corruption, undermining democratic equality..

quizQuestions.question: According to John Locke, the primary purpose of government is to:
options: Protect natural rights of life, liberty, and property
quizQuestions.answer: Protect natural rights of life, liberty, and property
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Protect natural rights of life, liberty, and property.

quizQuestions.question: Thomas Hobbes described life without government as:
options: Solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short
quizQuestions.answer: Solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short.

quizQuestions.question: John Locke's political theory most directly influenced which idea in the Declaration of Independence?
options: Governments derive just powers from the consent of the governed
The president should serve a lifetime term
quizQuestions.answer: Governments derive just powers from the consent of the governed
quizQuestions.explanation: Locke's ideas about natural rights and consent influenced the Declaration's claim that legitimate government rests on the consent of the governed.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Which type of government is defined by rule by a small, elite group?
quizQuestions.answer: Oligarchy
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "Oligarchy" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: The Constitutional Convention was held in:
quizQuestions.answer: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.

quizQuestions.question: The Preamble to the Constitution begins with the words:
options: 'We the People of the United States…'
'When in the course of human events…'
quizQuestions.answer: 'We the People of the United States…'
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: 'We the People of the United States…'.

quizQuestions.question: The Three-Fifths Compromise counted enslaved people as:
options: Three-fifths of a person for representation and taxation
Equal to free citizens only for taxation
quizQuestions.answer: Three-fifths of a person for representation and taxation
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Three-fifths of a person for representation and taxation.

quizQuestions.question: The Constitutional Convention of 1787 was held in:
quizQuestions.answer: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
quizQuestions.explanation: Delegates met in Philadelphia to revise the Articles of Confederation and ultimately draft the Constitution.

quizQuestions.question: The First Amendment protects all of the following EXCEPT:
quizQuestions.answer: Freedom from unreasonable searches
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Freedom from unreasonable searches.

quizQuestions.question: In Tinker v. Des Moines (1969), the Supreme Court ruled that:
options: Students do not lose First Amendment rights at school
Flag burning is not protected speech
Prayer in public schools is constitutional
quizQuestions.answer: Students do not lose First Amendment rights at school
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Students do not lose First Amendment rights at school.

quizQuestions.question: The Fifth Amendment right against self-incrimination means:
options: You cannot be arrested without a warrant
You cannot be forced to testify against yourself
You have the right to an attorney
quizQuestions.answer: You cannot be forced to testify against yourself
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: You cannot be forced to testify against yourself.

quizQuestions.question: The U.S. Congress is bicameral, meaning it has:
options: Two chambers — the House and the Senate
quizQuestions.answer: Two chambers — the House and the Senate
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Two chambers — the House and the Senate.

quizQuestions.question: House of Representatives members serve terms of:
quizQuestions.answer: Two years
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Two years.

quizQuestions.question: After a bill passes both chambers of Congress, it is sent to:
options: The president for signature or veto
quizQuestions.answer: The president for signature or veto
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: The president for signature or veto.

quizQuestions.question: Congressional committees are important because they:
options: Have the power to veto presidential actions
Review, revise, and decide the fate of most bills
quizQuestions.answer: Review, revise, and decide the fate of most bills
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Review, revise, and decide the fate of most bills.

quizQuestions.question: The president's role as Commander-in-Chief means:
options: The president can declare war independently
The president leads the armed forces but Congress declares war
The president outranks all military officers in combat
The military can override presidential orders
quizQuestions.answer: The president leads the armed forces but Congress declares war
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: The president leads the armed forces but Congress declares war.

quizQuestions.question: The Cabinet is composed of:
options: Heads of executive departments and key advisors appointed by the president
Senior members of Congress from both parties
quizQuestions.answer: Heads of executive departments and key advisors appointed by the president
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Heads of executive departments and key advisors appointed by the president.

quizQuestions.question: The president's role as Commander in Chief means that the president:
options: Leads the armed forces, while Congress has the power to declare war
Serves as chief justice of the Supreme Court
quizQuestions.answer: Leads the armed forces, while Congress has the power to declare war
quizQuestions.explanation: The Constitution makes the president commander in chief of the armed forces, while Congress holds the power to declare war.

quizQuestions.question: The president's Cabinet is mainly composed of:
options: Heads of executive departments and other senior advisors
All members of the Supreme Court
Leaders chosen directly by the House minority party
quizQuestions.answer: Heads of executive departments and other senior advisors
quizQuestions.explanation: The Cabinet includes executive department heads and other senior advisors who help the president administer the executive branch.

quizQuestions.question: The federal court system has how many levels?
options: Three (district courts, courts of appeals, Supreme Court)
Four (local, state, federal circuit, Supreme Court)
quizQuestions.answer: Three (district courts, courts of appeals, Supreme Court)
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Three (district courts, courts of appeals, Supreme Court).

quizQuestions.question: Judicial review was established in which landmark case?
quizQuestions.answer: Marbury v. Madison
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Marbury v. Madison.

quizQuestions.question: Federal judges are appointed by:
options: The Chief Justice of the Supreme Court
quizQuestions.answer: The president with Senate confirmation
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: The president with Senate confirmation.

quizQuestions.question: The federal court system is commonly described as having which three main levels?
options: Municipal courts, state courts, and tribal courts
District courts, courts of appeals, and the Supreme Court
Town councils, state legislatures, and Congress
Trial juries, grand juries, and electoral colleges
quizQuestions.answer: District courts, courts of appeals, and the Supreme Court
quizQuestions.explanation: The main federal court levels are district courts, courts of appeals, and the Supreme Court.

quizQuestions.question: Powers held exclusively by the federal government are called:
quizQuestions.answer: Enumerated (delegated) powers
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Enumerated (delegated) powers.

quizQuestions.question: The Tenth Amendment reserves non-delegated powers to:
quizQuestions.answer: The states or the people
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: The states or the people.

quizQuestions.question: Powers held exclusively by the federal government are often called:
quizQuestions.answer: Enumerated or delegated powers
quizQuestions.explanation: Enumerated or delegated powers are powers assigned to the federal government by the Constitution.

quizQuestions.question: The United States has a two-party system largely because of:
options: A constitutional requirement for two parties
A Supreme Court ruling mandating two parties
quizQuestions.answer: Winner-take-all (plurality) voting in single-member districts
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Winner-take-all (plurality) voting in single-member districts.

quizQuestions.question: A party platform is:
options: The physical stage at a party convention
A formal statement of a party's goals and policy positions
The list of candidates running for office
quizQuestions.answer: A formal statement of a party's goals and policy positions
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: A formal statement of a party's goals and policy positions.

quizQuestions.question: In the Electoral College, a candidate needs how many electoral votes to win the presidency?
quizQuestions.answer: 270 out of 538
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: 270 out of 538.

quizQuestions.question: Proportional representation would most likely affect the party system by:
options: Making it easier for smaller parties to win seats
Requiring every state to have exactly two parties
quizQuestions.answer: Making it easier for smaller parties to win seats
quizQuestions.explanation: Proportional representation can give parties seats based on vote share, which usually makes it easier for smaller parties to gain representation.

quizQuestions.question: Which factor is most consistently associated with higher voter turnout?
quizQuestions.answer: Higher education and income levels
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Higher education and income levels.

quizQuestions.question: The Citizens United v. FEC Supreme Court decision ruled that:
options: Campaign donations to candidates are unlimited
Corporations and groups can spend unlimited money on independent political ads
Foreign nationals can donate to U.S. campaigns
Public funding of elections is unconstitutional
quizQuestions.answer: Corporations and groups can spend unlimited money on independent political ads
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Corporations and groups can spend unlimited money on independent political ads.

quizQuestions.question: Gerrymandering most directly affects:
options: The drawing of congressional district boundaries for partisan advantage
quizQuestions.answer: The drawing of congressional district boundaries for partisan advantage
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: The drawing of congressional district boundaries for partisan advantage.

quizQuestions.question: The first stage of the public policy process is:
options: Agenda setting — identifying which problems government will address
quizQuestions.answer: Agenda setting — identifying which problems government will address
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Agenda setting — identifying which problems government will address.

quizQuestions.question: Interest groups influence public policy primarily by:
options: Lobbying lawmakers, funding campaigns, and mobilizing voters
Declaring war on behalf of the government
quizQuestions.answer: Lobbying lawmakers, funding campaigns, and mobilizing voters
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Lobbying lawmakers, funding campaigns, and mobilizing voters.

quizQuestions.question: Policy implementation is carried out primarily by:
quizQuestions.answer: Federal and state bureaucratic agencies
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Federal and state bureaucratic agencies.

quizQuestions.question: Policy evaluation asks:
options: Did the policy achieve its intended goals effectively?
How much did the policy cost to draft?
quizQuestions.answer: Did the policy achieve its intended goals effectively?
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Did the policy achieve its intended goals effectively?.

quizQuestions.question: The 14th Amendment (1868) was primarily intended to:
options: Grant citizenship and equal protection to formerly enslaved people
Give women the right to vote
quizQuestions.answer: Grant citizenship and equal protection to formerly enslaved people
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Grant citizenship and equal protection to formerly enslaved people.

quizQuestions.question: The Civil Rights Act of 1964 was significant because it:
options: Gave Black Americans the right to vote
Banned segregation in public places and discrimination in employment
quizQuestions.answer: Banned segregation in public places and discrimination in employment
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Banned segregation in public places and discrimination in employment.

quizQuestions.question: The Fourteenth Amendment was primarily intended after the Civil War to:
options: Secure citizenship and equal protection for formerly enslaved people
Limit the president to two terms
quizQuestions.answer: Secure citizenship and equal protection for formerly enslaved people
quizQuestions.explanation: The Fourteenth Amendment established citizenship and equal protection principles during Reconstruction.

quizQuestions.question: The Voting Rights Act of 1965 was significant because it:
options: Targeted discriminatory voting practices such as literacy tests
Created the direct election of senators
Required all states to use proportional representation
quizQuestions.answer: Targeted discriminatory voting practices such as literacy tests
quizQuestions.explanation: The Voting Rights Act of 1965 gave the federal government stronger tools to challenge discriminatory voting barriers, especially in states with histories of suppression.

quizQuestions.question: The primary tools of U.S. foreign policy include all EXCEPT:
quizQuestions.answer: Domestic tax policy
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Domestic tax policy.

quizQuestions.question: NATO's Article 5 states that:
options: All members must maintain a standing army
An attack on one member is considered an attack on all members
Members cannot engage in trade with non-NATO countries
The United States leads all NATO military operations
quizQuestions.answer: An attack on one member is considered an attack on all members
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: An attack on one member is considered an attack on all members.

quizQuestions.question: The United Nations Security Council has the power to:
options: Authorize international peacekeeping missions and sanctions
quizQuestions.answer: Authorize international peacekeeping missions and sanctions
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Authorize international peacekeeping missions and sanctions.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is not primarily a tool of U.S. foreign policy?
quizQuestions.answer: Domestic tax policy
quizQuestions.explanation: Diplomacy, treaties, and sanctions are common foreign policy tools; domestic tax policy is primarily an internal economic policy tool.

quizQuestions.question: Fiscal policy refers to the government's use of:
options: Interest rates set by the Federal Reserve
Taxation and government spending to manage the economy
quizQuestions.answer: Taxation and government spending to manage the economy
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Taxation and government spending to manage the economy.

quizQuestions.question: The federal budget process begins when:
options: The Supreme Court approves annual spending
The president submits a budget proposal to Congress
The Treasury Department sets tax rates
The Senate Finance Committee votes on spending caps
quizQuestions.answer: The president submits a budget proposal to Congress
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: The president submits a budget proposal to Congress.

quizQuestions.question: Monetary policy differs from fiscal policy because monetary policy primarily involves:
options: Central bank actions affecting interest rates and the money supply
quizQuestions.answer: Central bank actions affecting interest rates and the money supply
quizQuestions.explanation: Fiscal policy uses taxing and spending. Monetary policy is mainly conducted by the Federal Reserve through tools that influence credit, interest rates, and money supply.
